m=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) a 24-year-old girlfriend, he once infamously derid=
ed social media as =E2=80=9CSnapface.=E2=80=9D

Several sources told me that John Preyer, an environmental advocate and the=
 school=E2=80=99s board chair, was the driving force behind the Belichick g=
amble and convinced Cunningham to sign off on the idea. For his part, Cunni=
ngham told ( https://www.wsj.com/sports/football/bill-belichick-unc-north-c=
arolina-coaching-openings-013fb5a7?utm_campaign=3DThe+Varsity+-+SUBSCRIBERS=
+%2812%2F12%2F24%29&utm_content=3DThe+Varsity+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%2812%2F12%2F24=
%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617d=
a42abd12a ) The Wall Street Journal that the hire =E2=80=9Crepresents a new=
 approach. =E2=80=A6 We know that college athletics is changing, and those =
changes require new and innovative thinking.=E2=80=9D Indeed, Belichick=E2=
=80=99s first move was to install former NFL executive Mike Lombardi, who s=
imilarly has no experience in the modern college game, as his general manag=
er. (Up until the Tar Heels came calling, Lombardi and Belichick were hosti=
ng a podcast, Coach, along with former Patriots
assistant Matt Patricia.)

Indeed, Chapel Hill may soon look like Foxboro South=E2=80=94a college prog=
ram run by NFL expats who were umbilically
raised on =E2=80=9CThe Patriot Way.=E2=80=9D ESPN=E2=80=99s Troy Aikman flo=
ated the notion on Pardon the Interruption that many in the industry suspec=
t Belichick will install his son, Stephen, currently the defensive coordina=
tor at the University of Washington, on his staff as a veritable successor-=
in-waiting. It also wouldn=E2=80=99t be surprising to see Belichick round u=
p other figures from the Patriots glory years, few of whom found profession=
al success outside Foxboro and all of whom would be available to take the c=
all=E2=80=94Patricia, perhaps, but also Josh McDaniels, Joe Judge, etcetera=
=2E (In a past life, and in more provin=
cial times, former Patriots offensive=20=
coordinator Charlie Weis illustrated the challenge of pivoting from the pro=
s to college. Despite two spectacular seasons after his arrival in South Be=
nd, Weis=E2=80=99s Notre Dame tenure only lasted five years.)

Anyway, the Belichick hire evidences the mounting professionalization of co=
llege sports, but also the extraordinary money at stake and the crafty deal=
 structures afoot. Florida State recently negotiated to sell a stake in its=
 athletics program to private equity company Sixth Street. (Those talks wen=
t nowhere, but still=E2=80=A6) The Big 12 has talked about selling naming r=
ights to its conference to various companies, though nothing has come of th=
ose talks yet. Can you imagine playing in the State Farm 12 conference?

The plenitude of private equity dry powder lurking around the college game =
dominated the SBJ conference in Las Vegas this week. =E2=80=9CThis is not a=
n if, it=E2=80=99s a when,=E2=80=9D said American Athletic Conference commi=
ssioner Tim Pernetti. =E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s essential to bring partnerships=
 in to help us achieve these goals.
But everybody has got to get a grip that it=E2=80=99s changing under our fe=
et. So we can keep tap dancing and hoping that it stops, or we could actual=
ly move with it, but we=E2=80=99re behind it.=E2=80=9D

The Coach Prime Playbook

Belichick will likely commercialize Chapel Hill=E2=80=94long a backwater fo=
r college football=E2=80=94in the same way that Deion Sanders almost single=
-handedly resurrected the University of Colorado. Two years ago, after Colo=
rado hired Sanders, a.k.a. Coach Prime, Fox Sports rolled out a marketing c=
ampaign focused on the new coach=E2=80=99s larger-than-life personality in =
order to support its Big 12 rights package.

Even though the team had been dreadful for years, Fox Sports bet big on Dei=
on. Executives made sure to put Colorado in its main window for its first t=
wo games, and sent its pregame show to both. The Buffaloes=E2=80=99 opening=
 win against a nationally ranked TCU team instantaneously rendered the risk=
y bid a total masterstroke. The game, played in sweltering conditions, drew=
 more than 7 million viewers. A couple of weeks
later, ESPN drew more than 9 million viewers for a Colorado-Colorado State =
overtime game.

There are only a handful of college coaches who can generate television rat=
ings. For a time, when he first arrived at Michigan, Fox identified Jim Har=
baugh as another needle-mover, the term de arte in some circles. ESPN, whic=
h holds the rights to all ACC games, will likely run this same playbook, al=
beit on steroids. The opener=E2=80=94also against TCU, ironically=E2=80=94i=
s scheduled for Labor Day weekend, and historically, ESPN has scheduled an =
ACC game in primetime on Monday night of that weekend. So it seems likely t=
hat it would move Belichick=E2=80=99s first game into that slot.

Sources in Bristol seem as excited about the prospect of Belichick coming t=
o college football as Fox was about Colorado hiring Deion, and they will ce=
rtainly give UNC some of the best windows early in the season. The problem =
is that UNC=E2=80=99s early-season schedule is not particularly
strong, with Charlotte, Richmond, and UCF following TCU. Plus, ESPN holds a=
ll the rights to the far better SEC, so it=E2=80=99s unlikely that UNC will=
 become more than an interloper on ABC=E2=80=99s primetime Saturday window=
=E2=80=94usually the demesne of powerhouse teams like Georgia, Alabama, Tex=
as, Tennessee, etcetera.

But ESPN has plenty of not-quite-primetime windows across its networks, and=
 I=E2=80=99m told that UNC will be in heavy rotation for one of the ABC win=
dows most weeks, as long as the team wins some games. =E2=80=9CThis is wher=
e the casual sports fan and the casual college football fan enters the equa=
tion,=E2=80=9D one source explained. =E2=80=9CThat incremental audience is =
not necessarily avid college football fans. It=E2=80=99s people who want to=
 see Belichick coach college football.=E2=80=9D

From the Cheap Seats

On the prospects of a hard MLB salary cap: =E2=80=9CThere=E2=80=99s one pro=
blem with your musing about a salary cap in baseball: A smart owner would k=
now that it will probably mean a lost season, and what was the lesson of 19=
94? The fans never come back. Baseball is an everyday sport, and if it=E2=
=80=99s not going to be there, a lot of people will discover other ways to =
fill that time and/or get angry about millionaires and billionaires fightin=
g over money and depriving them of the game. The result will be that the ow=
ners kill the golden goose. A smarter answer might be yet another tier of l=
uxury tax, or maybe even a tax on an individual contract over a certain amo=
unt.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94A Varsity subscriber

More on the salary cap: =E2=80=9CThe only way a salary cap might actually c=
ome about is if those same small-market owners conceded to establishing a
salary floor (along with many other concessions, probably). So what do John=
 Fisher and his ilk care about more=E2=80=94getting their asses kicked on a=
 yearly basis, or actually being forced to open up their wallets?=E2=80=9D =
=E2=80=94A Varsity subscriber

as a reminder of what an amazing human being he is. When I was 22 and had j=
ust graduated from the University of Minnesota, I took a one-week trip to N=
ew York to try and find a job in sports media. I somehow figured out how to=
 send an email to Sean, and he was kind enough to give me 15 minutes of his=
 time. I'll never forget
that, and you could hear the humanity in his voice throughout your podcast,=
 including his recall of the Tiger Woods moments, and how he went about hir=
ing people.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94A media executive

Have a great weekend,
John

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">You might think tha=
t MLB teams would be increasingly <i>frugal</i> this offseason, especially =
considering that the reliable piles of money they have long received from t=
heir regional sports network partners appear to be blowing away. Similarly,=
 MLB is likely to take a haircut on its <i>Sunday Night Baseball </i>arrang=
ement with ESPN, and <b>David Zaslav </b>suggested this morning that he=E2=
=80=99ll soon be sending longtime partners TBS and TNT to their own SpinCo-=
style never-never land. But where most dealmakers see belt-tightening, uber=
-agent <b>Scott Boras</b> sees only potential. Boras deftly convinced the l=
eague=E2=80=99s big-money clubs to negotiate against each other, leading to=
 <b>Juan Soto</b>=E2=80=99s record-setting 15-year, $765 million deal with =
the Mets. Boras=E2=80=99s other free agent clients (<b>Pete Alonso</b>, <b>=
Alex Bregman</b>, <b>Corbin Burnes</b>,
<b>Sean Manaea</b>) are poised to break the bank, too.</td>

         <tr class=3D"replica" name=3D"s1_v2">
          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Light a candle for =
MLS commish <b>Don Garber</b>. The MLS playoffs had everything a profession=
al sports league and its commissioner could want: Teams representing the co=
untry=E2=80=99s two largest markets=E2=80=94the L.A. Galaxy and the New Yor=
k Red Bulls=E2=80=94both made the finals. Saturday=E2=80=99s game, held in =
the entertainment capital of L.A., was actually close: The Galaxy defeated =
the Red Bulls 2-1. And yet, the TV ratings were <i>abysmal</i>, averaging j=
ust 468,000 viewers on both Fox and Fox Deportes. Apple, which streamed the=
 game, doesn=E2=80=99t release viewer figures, but <i>all </i>of Apple TV+ =
averaged just 287,000 viewers during the MLS Cup time slot on game night, p=
er Nielsen=E2=80=99s Streaming Platform Ratings. <br/><br/>For context, som=
e 222,000 people were watching Apple TV+ during the same window the previou=
s Saturday, when no live MLS match was being
streamed, suggesting that MLS Cup led to an incremental gain of just 65,000=
 viewers. In the hour before the MLS Cup, total Apple TV+ viewership was at=
 251,000. Then, in the hour <i>after</i> the MLS Cup, that figure was <i>up=
</i> to 385,000. Alas, more than a year after <b>Messi </b>promised his own=
 form of pink-jerseyed Caitlinsanity, the MLS audience for its championship=
 game was down nearly <i>50 percent</i> from last year, when Fox and Fox De=
portes pulled in 890,000 viewers combined. MLS is two years into its 10-yea=
r, $2.5 billion deal with Apple.<br/></td>

:0;color:#000000;font-size:16px;line-height:30px;margin-top:0;margin-bottom=
:0;list-style-type:decimal"><li><i>A non-update FIFA update</i>: The year=
=E2=80=99s biggest sports media mystery is centered on who actually won the=
 U.S. rights to carry the Women=E2=80=99s World Cup in 2027 and 2031. As I =
recently noted, FIFA told the traditional media bidders=E2=80=94CBS, Fox, a=
nd NBC=E2=80=94that it=E2=80=99s currently in exclusive negotiations with a=
n unnamed <i>non-traditional</i> media company, which many hypothesize to b=
e DAZN, a British streaming platform which recently picked up Club World Cu=
p global rights for a cool billion. Originally, Fox and Telemundo executive=
s were told that they were next in line for the event=E2=80=99s English- an=
d Spanish-language rights should those exclusive talks fall apart. <br/><br=
/>Here=E2=80=99s another wrinkle in this micro-drama: I=E2=80=99ve
been told that FIFA was willing to sell the rights to this non-traditional =
media company if, and <i>only </i>if, it agreed to sublicense some of the g=
ames to a traditional media entity. You know, like a broadcast network. So =
while DAZN lays out some serious scratch for these games, it will end up lo=
sing its exclusivity on most of the marquee matches in the process. It=E2=
=80=99ll be interesting to see how this turns out for the linear networks, =
one of whom may be able to get the games they want=E2=80=94albeit without t=
otal exclusivity=E2=80=94at a discount. <br/><br/></li><li><i>YouTube TV=E2=
=80=99s cable moment</i>: YouTube TV has officially crossed a familiar thre=
shold as an M.V.P.D.=E2=80=94blaming rising programming costs for its price=
 hikes. In today=E2=80=99s letter to subscribers, YouTube declared: =E2=80=
=9CTo keep up with the rising cost of content and the investments we make i=
n the quality of our service, we=E2=80=99re updating our monthly price from=
 $72.99/month to $82.99/month starting January 13,
2025.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/>That =E2=80=9Crising cost of content=E2=80=9D certa=
inly includes the seven-year, $14 billion deal that YouTube TV signed two y=
ears ago to carry NFL Sunday Ticket. YouTube TV agreed to shell out $2 bill=
ion annually partly because of the potential cost savings it would realize =
on some of its programming contracts if it picked up enough subscribers to =
leapfrog Dish Network and become the fourth-largest M.V.P.D., sources told =
me. (Historically, network groups have offered better rates to the bigger d=
istributors.) At the end of last year, YouTube was indeed the fourth-larges=
t M.V.P.D., behind Comcast, Spectrum, and DirecTV. And yet=E2=80=A6<br/><br=
/></li><li><i>The Zaz chronicles</i>: Warner Bros. Discovery=E2=80=99s deci=
sion to separate its company into two parts, Global Linear Networks and Str=
eaming & Studios, elicited a smart take from MoffettNathanson=E2=80=99s=
 <b>Robert Fishman</b>. =E2=80=9CNothing in the announcement changes anythi=
ng core to WBD=E2=80=99s business,=E2=80=9D Fishman wrote in a report
published this afternoon. =E2=80=9CThe company will still rely on linear ne=
twork cash flows to fund de-levering and continued D.T.C. investment.=E2=80=
=9D<br/><br/>In many ways, <b>David Zaslav</b>=E2=80=99s restructuring rese=
mbles Comcast=E2=80=99s recent decision to spin off its cable networks into=
 a separate company that will be run by <b>Mark Lazarus</b>. (My Puck partn=
er <b>Matt Belloni</b>, a former attorney, has referred to the spun-off net=
works as CrapCo.) Similarly, WBD=E2=80=99s pivot separates the company=E2=
=80=99s growth assets from its declining linear TV networks. =E2=80=9CWhile=
 the move falls short of the full spinout of cable network assets Comcast r=
ecently initiated, it is nevertheless another move to highlight the optiona=
lity and flexibility of these two increasingly divergent businesses,=E2=80=
=9D Fishman wrote. Let=E2=80=99s see which domino falls next=E2=80=A6<br/><=
br/></li><li><i>Big Ten deals</i>: NBC Sports <i>still</i> hasn=E2=80=99t s=
igned its media rights contract with the Big Ten, even though it=E2=80=99s =
in the second
year of the conference=E2=80=99s seven-year arrangement. (The Big Ten has m=
edia rights deals with CBS, Fox, and NBC.) Complicating matters, those righ=
ts are held by the Big Ten Network, which is majority-owned by Fox, the ent=
ity that managed the negotiations with its partner-rival networks. <i>The W=
ashington Post</i>=E2=80=99s <b>Ben Strauss</b>, who has done a <i>ton </i>=
of reporting on this topic, made a few fascinating points about the deal on=
 the<i> Varsity </i><u><a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZ=
CI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dUdmdORjF3WHpxMGdZazhaMG1XST0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0=
cHM6Ly9wdWNrLm5ld3MvcG9kY2FzdF9lcGlzb2RlL3N0ZXBoZW4tYS1icmVha3MtdGhlLWJhbms=
td29ybGQtY3VwLW1lZGlhLWxpbWJvLz91dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249VGhlK1ZhcnNpdHkrLStTVUJTQ1=

1NVQlNDUklCRVJTKyUyODEyJTJGMTIlMkYyNCUyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWxfYWN0=
aW9uXHUwMDI2dXRtX3NvdXJjZT1jdXN0b21lci5pb1x1MDAyNnV0bV90ZXJtPWY2YzYwNjE3ZGE=
0MmFiZDEyYSIsImludGVybmFsIjoiZjZjNjA2MTdkYTQyYWJkMTJhIiwibGlua19pZCI6MjAxND=
g4Mn0/6787f38a322387d178cdf249b88b0c8a3fba6cb686673126456fdbdcf028d6a3" sty=
le=3D"color:#d40000;text-decoration:none;font-weight:bold" class=3D"sdf">po=
dcast</a></u>: =E2=80=9CThis is not unprecedented. It=E2=80=99s my understa=
nding that CBS and the S.E.C. never signed their full, long-form contract, =
either. Money still changes hands. Games are still on television. But it al=
lows for way
more haggling and arguing over what is owed. What NBC has expressed to othe=
r people is that they didn=E2=80=99t realize the extent that Big Ten teams =
don=E2=80=99t want to play night games.=E2=80=9D<br/><br/>My take: I have b=
ad news for the Ohio State, Michigan, Penn State, and Wisconsin fans who ar=
e tired of having so many noon kickoffs: That=E2=80=99s not changing anytim=
e soon. The universities don=E2=80=99t want it to change. Fox, a couple of =
years ago, set up its noon kickoff as their main window. There was little c=
ompetition because everybody else scheduled the worst games at noon, and Fo=
x ended up having the highest-rated window for several years. The conferenc=
e is in on that strategy, and those main teams in the conference are in on =
that strategy.</li><br/><li><i>R.I.P. David Bonderman</i>: The Seattle Krak=
en owner, who died Wednesday, made his name and fortune in private equity a=
s the co-founder of TPG. But <b>Bonderman</b>, a longtime environmentalist =
and former environmental attorney, also
helped shape his legacy through his stewardship of the NHL team. Among othe=
r things, he helped erect one of the world=E2=80=99s first carbon-neutral a=
renas, the Climate Pledge Arena, in his hometown. (Disclosure: TPG is an in=
vestor in Puck.)</li></ol></td>

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Anyway, the Belichi=
ck hire evidences the mounting professionalization of college sports, but a=
lso the <i>extraordinary </i>money at stake and the crafty deal structures =
afoot. Florida State recently negotiated to sell a stake in its athletics p=
rogram to private equity company Sixth Street. (Those talks went nowhere, b=
ut still=E2=80=A6) The Big 12 has talked about selling naming rights to its=
 conference to various companies, though nothing has come of those talks ye=
t. Can you imagine playing in the State Farm 12 conference?<br/><br/>The pl=
enitude of private equity dry powder lurking around the college game domina=
ted the SBJ conference in Las Vegas this week. =E2=80=9CThis is not an <i>i=
f</i>, it=E2=80=99s a <i>when</i>,=E2=80=9D said American Athletic Conferen=
ce commissioner <b>Tim Pernetti</b>. =E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s essential to bri=
ng partnerships in to help us achieve these goals.
But everybody has got to get a grip that it=E2=80=99s changing under our fe=
et. So we can keep tap dancing and hoping that it stops, or we could actual=
ly move with it, but we=E2=80=99re behind it.=E2=80=9D<br/></td>

          <td style=3D"padding-bottom: 36px; color: #000000; font-size: 16p=
x; line-height: 30px; margin-top: 0; margin-bottom: 0;">Belichick will like=
ly commercialize Chapel Hill=E2=80=94long a backwater for college football=
=E2=80=94in the same way that <b>Deion Sanders</b> almost single-handedly r=
esurrected the University of Colorado. Two years ago, after Colorado hired =
Sanders, a.k.a. <i>Coach Prime</i>, Fox Sports rolled out a marketing campa=
ign focused on the new coach=E2=80=99s larger-than-life personality in orde=
r to support its Big 12 rights package. <br/><br/>Even though the team had =
been dreadful for <i>years</i>, Fox Sports bet big on Deion. Executives mad=
e sure to put Colorado in its main window for its first two games, and sent=
 its pregame show to both. The Buffaloes=E2=80=99 opening win against a nat=
ionally ranked TCU team <i>instantaneously </i>rendered the risky bid a tot=
al masterstroke. The game, played in sweltering conditions, drew more than =
7 million viewers. A couple of weeks
later, ESPN drew more than 9 million viewers for a Colorado-Colorado State =
overtime game. <br/><br/>There are only a handful of college coaches who ca=
n generate television ratings. For a time, when he first arrived at Michiga=
n, Fox identified <b>Jim Harbaugh</b> as another <i>needle-mover</i>, the <=
i>term de arte </i>in some circles. ESPN, which holds the rights to all ACC=
 games, will likely run this same playbook, albeit on steroids. The opener=
=E2=80=94also against TCU, ironically=E2=80=94is scheduled for Labor Day we=
ekend, and historically, ESPN has scheduled an ACC game in primetime on Mon=
day night of that weekend. So it seems likely that it would move Belichick=
=E2=80=99s first game into that slot.<br/><br/>Sources in Bristol seem as e=
xcited about the prospect of Belichick coming to college football as Fox wa=
s about Colorado hiring Deion, and they will certainly give UNC some of the=
 best windows early in the season. The problem is that UNC=E2=80=99s early-=
season schedule is not particularly
strong, with Charlotte, Richmond, and UCF following TCU. Plus, ESPN holds a=
ll the rights to the far better SEC, so it=E2=80=99s unlikely that UNC will=
 become more than an interloper on ABC=E2=80=99s primetime Saturday window=
=E2=80=94usually the demesne of powerhouse teams like Georgia, Alabama, Tex=
as, Tennessee, etcetera. <br/><br/>But ESPN has plenty of <i>not-quite-prim=
etime</i> windows across its networks, and I=E2=80=99m told that UNC will b=
e in heavy rotation for one of the ABC windows most weeks, as long as the t=
eam wins some games. =E2=80=9CThis is where the casual sports fan and the c=
asual college football fan enters the equation,=E2=80=9D one source explain=
ed. =E2=80=9CThat incremental audience is not necessarily avid college foot=
ball fans. It=E2=80=99s people who want to see <i>Belichick</i> coach colle=
ge football.=E2=80=9D<br/></td>
